text: I'd like to build an application to help the APS engage creatively with AI. Like a brainstorming thought partner

That sounds like a fantastic initiative! Helping public servants get comfortable and creative with AI is a great way to build internal capacity. To help me get a better sense of the scope, could you tell me a bit more about the specific problem this 'thought partner' is trying to solve? For example, are public servants struggling to draft policy, brainstorm project ideas, or perhaps synthesize complex research?
it's to help them come up with creative ways they can solve problems. They don't know what they don't know. This would help to solve that

That makes a lot of sense—the 'unknown unknowns' are often the biggest hurdle in public policy design. It sounds like you're aiming to bridge that gap by providing a spark for lateral thinking. To help me flesh out the 'who' behind this, could you tell me a bit more about the intended users? Are you imagining this for policy officers, project managers, or perhaps a broader group across the APS?

Basically anyone within the APS. I hope to make it available through th Department of Finance's GovAI Exchange platform. They support up to OFFICAL information on that platform so I think it would be a good place to start at least

That sounds like a great strategy—leveraging an existing, trusted platform like the GovAI Exchange is a smart way to ensure accessibility and compliance from the start. Since we're targeting a broad APS audience and working with OFFICIAL information, I'm curious about the data side of things. When a user is brainstorming, what kind of information are they likely to input into the tool? Are they feeding it existing policy documents, project briefs, or just rough notes and ideas?
It's supposed to be a pretty broad use case so its hard to say exactly. I think it'd be safe to say that it wouldn't be anything sensitive

That makes sense—keeping it focused on non-sensitive, OFFICIAL information is a great way to lower the barrier to entry and encourage experimentation. Since the tool will be processing these rough notes and ideas to generate creative prompts, could you walk me through a 'happy path' for a user? For example, if a policy officer has a vague idea for a project, what does the interaction look like from the moment they open the tool until they get that 'aha!' moment?
probably a voice interaction. Something that feels like talking to a real human. One that takes their idea and builds on it or challenges assumptions. I'd want the UI to be clean

A voice-first interface is a really compelling choice for a brainstorming partner—it can make the process feel much more fluid and conversational, almost like a whiteboard session with a colleague. I've updated the outline to reflect that. To help me understand the practical side of this, what are the main alternatives you've considered? For instance, have you looked at existing off-the-shelf AI chat tools, and if so, what makes this specific 'thought partner' approach different or more effective for the APS?
I haven't really thought of anything else? Other than googling I don't see how a non-AI solution could feasibly fill this gap

That's a fair point—traditional search engines like Google are great for finding existing information, but they don't really help you synthesize or iterate on a new idea in the same way a conversational partner does. It sounds like the 'thought partner' aspect is the key differentiator here. Moving on to the practical side of things, are there any specific technical or organisational constraints we should keep in mind? For example, are there requirements around where the data is hosted, or perhaps specific accessibility standards you need to meet to ensure it's usable for all public servants?
Data needs to be hosted on Australian soil. It can't provide legal advice. I think it would be helpful to show HOW the system is thinking to reduce the liklihood of user's overrelying on the output

Those are crucial constraints—data sovereignty is a standard requirement for the APS, and building in 'explainability' is a very smart way to manage the risk of over-reliance. I've added those to the constraints section. To wrap up our outline, how would you measure the success of this tool six months after launch? For example, are you looking for a certain number of active users, qualitative feedback on the quality of ideas generated, or perhaps a reduction in the time it takes to move from a rough concept to a project brief?
